Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered even though I put in decent hours. I'd usually block out time in the evenings, maybe three hours at a stretch, but I wasn't always mentally present during those sessions. I relied heavily on visual materials—diagrams, videos, charts—because that's how things click for me. Some weeks I'd be really on top of things, other weeks I'd just go through the motions. It's hard to explain, but even when I was studying, part of me felt disconnected from it all.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. When deadlines approached, I'd suddenly become very focused and cram in extra hours. But during quieter weeks, I'd let things slide and study whenever I felt like it, which wasn't always consistent. I tried to maintain some regularity, but life has a way of disrupting plans. Being 24 and dealing with stuff outside of school made it harder to stick to a rigid schedule.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just out of habit, scrolling through without really engaging with anything. Deadlines were the biggest prompt—if something was due soon, I'd be on there making sure I hadn't missed anything. Occasionally a peer would message me about something, and that would pull me back in. But I can't say I ever felt excited to log in.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee. It wasn't enthusiastic engagement, more like checking my phone for notifications—just something I did. The routine developed because I was taking so many online courses and needed to keep track of everything. It became part of my daily scroll, sandwiched between email and social media. But routine doesn't mean I was actually invested in what I was seeing.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched a lot of them—sometimes multiple times—because seeing concepts illustrated helped them stick. The downloadable slides and diagrams were great too because I could study them offline and annotate them. Discussion boards were less useful to me personally; I posted a few times but never felt like I got much out of them. Anything visual and structured worked best for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the optional readings and supplementary text materials. If it was a wall of text without visuals, I'd skim it at best or skip it entirely. The discussion forums felt like busywork to me, so I only posted when I absolutely had to. Some of the interactive quizzes I'd delay until the last minute because they felt tedious. I know these resources were there to help, but I just couldn't bring myself to prioritize them when I was already feeling low.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments. If watching a video or completing a task would clearly help my grade, I'd do it. Visual content that explained complex concepts efficiently always felt valuable. But anything that seemed like padding or wasn't directly tied to assessment felt like a waste. When you're managing multiple courses and dealing with personal stuff, you have to be strategic about where you put your energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, honestly. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they got done. Optional stuff always fell to the bottom of my priority list because there was no external pressure to complete them. I know that's probably not the best approach, but when you're feeling sad and unmotivated, you conserve energy for what's mandatory. The optional activities might have been enriching, but I never had the mental bandwidth to find out.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and videos, then study them on my tablet without being connected. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking. My notes were all handwritten because that helps me remember things visually. So there were hours of studying that the LMS would have no record of at all.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out materials and spread them across my desk, drawing diagrams and making connections by hand. I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times while taking notes, but none of that showed up online. Sometimes I'd study with a friend in person, working through problems together. The LMS only captures clicks and logins, not the actual cognitive work happening away from the screen. My best studying often happened with the laptop closed.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty common for me. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then never open it on the LMS again. All my subsequent studying with that material happened offline. It was more convenient to have everything saved locally so I could study on the bus or in places without good internet. The LMS might show I only viewed something once, but in reality I probably reviewed it five or six times offline.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging felt almost impossible. I've been dealing with some sadness that comes and goes, and during the low points, even logging in felt like a massive effort. Mid-semester was particularly rough—the workload piled up and I just felt overwhelmed and disconnected. The peer support intervention helped a bit, having someone check in on me, but the underlying heaviness was still there. Some days I'd stare at the screen and nothing would register.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS and just mindlessly click through things without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities—work stuff, family obligations—definitely pulled my attention away from the course. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely rather than confront what I didn't understand. The workload across eleven courses meant I was constantly triaging what got my real attention versus what got the bare minimum.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, thinking about personal stuff or just zoning out. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without reading a single word. There were sessions where I'd be logged in for an hour but couldn't tell you anything about what I'd supposedly studied. The LMS would show activity, but meaningful engagement? That's a different story entirely.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt performative and draining. Writing about my thoughts when I was feeling sad just seemed impossible. I'd also avoid starting assignments until close to the deadline because the task felt overwhelming from a distance. It's not that I didn't know they were important—I just couldn't muster the motivation until the pressure became unavoidable. Avoidance was a coping mechanism, even if it wasn't a healthy one.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be relatively quiet during the early parts of a module, then suddenly very active as due dates approached. The pressure of a deadline cut through my fog and forced me to engage. Without that external structure, I'd drift and procrastinate indefinitely. It's not ideal, but the deadline-driven approach got me through with decent grades even when my motivation was basically nonexistent.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback on an assignment would give me a temporary boost, like maybe I was doing okay after all. But if feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, it would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for a few days. I appreciated detailed feedback because it helped me improve, but emotionally it was hard to receive sometimes. The timing mattered too—quick feedback kept me engaged, delayed feedback made me feel disconnected.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me stay somewhat on track despite my tendency to drift. Having everything organized visually in the LMS made it easier to see what needed to be done. But the sheer volume of content across my eleven courses sometimes made the structure feel oppressive rather than helpful. Weekly modules worked better for me than having everything available at once, because it forced some pacing. Without structure, I would have been completely lost.
Q19: The visual organization of the course made engagement easier—I could see at a glance what was due and what resources were available. Video content being front and center helped since that's how I learn best. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks that felt like busywork. Also, discussion requirements felt forced and made me less likely to engage authentically. If the course had been more text-heavy, I probably would have struggled much more.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and constantly checking the LMS for updates or grade changes without actually producing work. Or maybe they're struggling to understand the material, so they keep reviewing resources but can't translate that into completed assignments. It could also be someone dealing with personal issues who's going through the motions of being a student without having the capacity to actually perform. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. My exam scores were really high even though my LMS activity probably looked moderate compared to some students. I did a lot of learning offline—downloaded materials, handwritten notes, external resources. Someone could appear disengaged online but be deeply engaged with the material in ways the system can't track. Performance and visible activity aren't the same thing, especially for students who learn differently.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. They're good at tracking completion of specific tasks and identifying students who might be falling behind on deadlines. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state of the student, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't tell if someone is actually absorbing material or just going through the motions. It's quantity data, not quality data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the course. It's when your mind is present, not just your clicks. For me, real engagement is rare—it's those moments when a concept suddenly makes sense and I feel genuinely interested. It's not about how many times I log in or how many videos I watch. It's about whether the learning matters to me in that moment. Lately, finding that feeling has been difficult.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I've been struggling with sadness throughout this course. It wouldn't capture the effort it takes just to show up some days, or how the peer support check-ins were sometimes the only thing keeping me accountable. It can't see that my high exam scores came from intense offline studying and a good memory, not from being genuinely engaged with the course community. The data shows a student doing fine; it doesn't show someone who's been drifting through, feeling disconnected from it all despite technically succeeding.
